{ Welcome to my book on chess endings. This is mostly aimed at my students, but is also freely available to anyone who can put up with the odd dad joke or two. Endings are often neglected in chess. They have the reputation for being boring. However, once you have played a few, you quickly realise that they can be frustrating and annoying as well. Hopefully this book is none of those things. And studying endgames has certainly helped raise my own standard of play when nothing else seemed to do the trick. To start off, a little study. White to play and draw. Shamelessly stole it from a friend's newsfeed. When I find the composer of the study I shall update this note accordingly. Have fun! }
1. Ng3 Bxg3 2. e7 Bd6+ 3. Ka4 Bxe7 4. Bc4 h1=Q 5. Bd5+ Qxd5 *

{ This was played in a minor, many many years ago. But it still remains my favourite game I have ever played. After a quiet opening where White was slightly better I broke prematurely with d5; the resulting simplifications should have been equal, but my opponent let me grab first one, and then the other of his bishops. In the resulting position the bishops were so superior to the knights that I was able to drive his exposed king out and mate him. Forcing mate at the end with Kg1 was particularly delicious. }

{ One of the most difficult decisions in chess is to navigate the piece exchanges. All exchanges are irreversible: the rules of the game do not allow us to bring back the exchanged pieces. Thus, exchanging is one of the most committal things you can do on a chessboard (similar to pawn moves or choosing the king's location in the middle game). Exchanges are critical and strategic decisions with long-term effects on the board. You might have heard the phrase: the art of exchanging pieces. Great champions such as Botvinnik, Smyslov, and Petrosian were well-known for their fine skill when it came to deciding on the correct exchange. In their hands, exchanging pieces could become a deadly weapon. This is also partly because of their fine endgame technique which allowed them to 'reduce' a complex position to a simpler won endgame through exchanging pieces.There is almost never an 'equal' exchange in chess: it is always one side or the other getting benefit from piece trades. With every exchange, several imbalances are created and new elements are introduced to the position. The correct judgment of these factors and their long-term effects makes the exchange an art form in chess. Exchanging pieces also has this wrong reputation of being 'cowardly': a boring player's way of releasing the tension and going into a dead-equal endgame. Nothing can be further from the truth! Not only because there is really no completely 'equal' exchange, but also because exchanges are in fact 'forcing' moves in chess: you force the opponent to react to your move, and we know that forcing moves are active moves! It also makes the calculation process easier: you can anticipate the resulting position after the piece trades. For all these reasons, exchanges should be rather regarded as 'exciting and active' types of moves in chess! In a nutshell, the decision on an exchange is never independent (e.g. I take your knight and you take my knight; hence it is an equal trade): it always depends on other factors in the position, i.e. the safety of the kings, the weaknesses in the position, who has more space, whose exchanged pieces were more active, who has a better endgame etc. This is why correct exchange is not easy: not only because exchanging is irreversible, but it also requires good judgment on several other factors which may sometimes contradict each other! Given these interdependent factors, carefully studying what makes a correct exchange can offer us enormous opportunities to improve our positional chess understanding. As we seek to understand the art of exchanging pieces, we must inevitably immerse ourselves with important concepts such as weak squares, active/passive pieces, king safety, pawn structure, space advantage, and endgame technique.In this course, we attempt to understand what makes a good exchange in chess, in relation to the concepts listed above. We discuss these concepts in this course in 10 different chapters. In the chapter The Final Test, the student will be tested over 60 positions where they need to decide on the correct exchange. My goal is not only to make you a better negotiator when it comes to exchanging opportunities but also to improve your overall chess understanding. I am a passionate chess reader, and I would like to acknowledge the following books which helped me during the creation of this course: “Mastering Chess Strategy” by Johan Hellsten has now become a chess classic, for good reasons. Johan is an amazing coach and author. He also used to play in my current chess club in Sweden. His books on strategy contain extremely instructive examples on exchanging pieces, some of which I used as well in this course
(with my own annotations)
.I also very much liked Axel Smith’s book “Pump Up Your Rating”. Axel talks about exchanging pieces in one chapter and gives great insights he gained on this topic from Ulf Andersson, a legendary Swedish player. Eduardas Rozentalis’ book “To Exchange or Not? The Ultimate Workbook” also contains several instructive positions on how to handle piece exchanges. Michael Stean’s “Simple Chess” is a very instructive book that talks about the benefits of piece trades in cramped positions. And I should also mention the Steps Method series by Brunia and van Wijgerden. Those books contain extremely instructive positions, distilled for their educational content.As in science, we are standing on the shoulder of giants in chess. The chess knowledge accumulates over time, with constant refinements and reinterpretations. I apologize if I missed out on acknowledging a particular book/author. I hope you will like the course! }
*

{ The chaptersThis course has 36 chapters: 1 introduction, 1 index, 1 basic test, 30 checkmate pattern chapters, 1 warm-up chapter, 1 exercises chapter and 1 final test. The index chapter contains 3 indices. The first one is an index of the mating patterns in this course. Click on a number behind the mating pattern to go to the corresponding section. The second one is an index with all the players whose games were used in this course. Click on a number after a player's name to go to the corresponding game of that player. The third one is an index with a timeline that lists the people on which some of the mating patterns are based. The point of doing this Basic Test first before you are given any instruction is not to solve all of the puzzles correctly the first time, although if you do that's great. But, the idea here is to help you identify which patterns you struggle with first. The names of the 30 checkmate pattern chapters and the informational sections are based on various sources I could find. My main sources were www.chessgames.com, www.wikipedia.org and www.chesstempo.com. Each of these 30 chapters has one or more short but instructive informational sections to give you an idea how a certain checkmate pattern can be executed. I based these sections on similarities that I saw among the many thousands of games I looked at. I do not claim that these are the only possibilities that lead to a certain type of checkmate. I mainly want to inspire you. The informational sections are followed by exercises, to put what has been learned into practice. The warm-up chapter contains over 200 puzzles, to practice all you have learned in the 30 checkmate pattern chapters. These are especially interesting for beginners and casual players, but these can also serve for intermediate players as a warm-up. More than 80% of these puzzles are mate-in-2 or mate-in-3. The exercises chapter contains nearly 300 puzzles, and takes all you have learned one step further. These puzzles may be too hard to find for beginners and casual players, they can be challenging for intermediate players but also interesting for advanced players. More than 75% of these puzzles are mate-in-3, mate-in-4 or mate-in-5. The final test contains 100 of the hardest puzzles in this course. The puzzlesThe title of each puzzle is the game on which it's based. This does not necessarily mean that the original game went exactly the same! You can view or replay each game from move 1. Each puzzle however starts at the moment the game can be ended with one of the checkmate patterns I describe in this course. Every solution ends with the name of the checkmate pattern on which that puzzle is based. In order to collect enough quality exercises for each pattern within a pattern, I sometimes took examples where e.g. a queen
(only using her diagonal ability)
, replaced a bishop. I decided not to mention this in any of the chapters, because it would complicate the basic patterns too much. Mate-in-X and alternative movesI tried to avoid using games with a dense forest of variations and lots of alternative moves. Each puzzle has one correct solution, and possibly a few alternative moves. The correct solution is
(unless otherwise stated)
the shortest possible mating sequence: a mate-in-X. The following types of moves are NOT accepted as an alternative move in this course: 1. The unnecessary repetition of moves, even if it is a mate-in-X according to engines; 2. Mate-in-2, mate-in-3,... in case there is a mate-in-1; 3. Silent moves that unnecessarily prolong the game, after which the game would still end with the same checkmate pattern as the shortest possible mating sequence; 4. Moves that will lead to checkmate in more than 15 moves
(with best play from both sides)
. This is a course about checkmate patterns, not about 'finding a good move'. All solutions are checked with an engine. If you believe that something isn’t correct, please leave a comment. Feel free to use an engine to better understand the solutions of the most difficult puzzles. Word of thanksThere are 1000 variations in this course, referring to the more than 1000 students who have purchased this course in the first months after publishing. I want to thank each of you for your support. Thanks to the fantastic testing work of skkeyan and superlychee, the level of this course in terms of content, usability and linguistics increased a lot. Thank you very much! And last but not least special thanks to my girlfriend Tania, for the patience she had during the 500+ hours I spent on making this course. Have fun! Raf Mesotten a.k.a. CraftyRaf }

solution:
{ Hello and welcome to The Chess Coach Companion: Intermediate Syllabus! This course has been created by some of the best coaches in the world in an effort to create a tool suitable for coaches to train their students, as well as chess enthusiasts working on their own. We have put a lot of time and effort into this project, and we are sure that you'll find it helpful. This material offers self-improvers valuable instruction from experts, and will save coaches countless hours of work when preparing their lessons! The syllabus explores topics of general training, strategic play, tactics, and endgame technique. Finally, we remind you that The Chess Coach Companion: Advanced Syllabus is also available on Chessable. There, you will find more material to bring your students' chess, or your own, even further! We hope this course will help you and/or your students improve your chess skills. Thank you for joining us! }
1. -- *

filename: The Fundamentals 1 Build Up Your Chess_firstkey.pgn
solution:
{ It was a pleasure to have Artur Yusupov working as my second, both personally and professionally. It is, therefore, an honour for me to write the preface to the new manual Build Up Your Chess with Artur Yusupov. This new book was created by expanding and improving the original online lessons from the Chess Tigers University. As an honorary member of the Chess Tigers, it has given me great pleasure to see this logical follow-up take concrete form and meet the twin challenges of being both a valuable textbook and a bedside book. It was in 1994 that I met Artur Yusupov in the semi-finals of the Candidates' cycle in Wijk aan Zee. I managed to come out ahead by 4.5-2.5, but I recognized that Artur harboured great potential, both in his chess knowledge and extensive match experience. Artur's systematic and professional approach to analysing games was the decisive factor in having him as my second in the World Championship Finals in New York 1995 and Lausanne 1998. His mastery of the methods of the Russian chess school was very helpful in the preparation for the matches, as well as during the matches themselves. It was his idea that I should play the Trompovsky in the last game in Lausanne. I was 3-2 down, but was able to level the match at 3-3 and thus force a play-off. I am still very grateful for everything that Artur did for me. Artur's vast experience as a trainer convinced him that there is a considerable need for better tuition for amateurs. Matching the level to the needs of the student is perhaps not too difficult, but the masterstroke is structuring the information in such a way that makes it immediately useful for amateurs. I am naturally enthusiastic about the rich variety of material in this series, which can help beginners become top amateurs. I wish Artur Yusupov all the best with the publication of the first book in the series Build Up Your Chess with Artur Yusupov. Making this work available in English means that even more people who are keen to learn can enjoy it to the full. World Champion, Viswanathan Anand }
1. -- *

{ When I first discussed the idea of The Ultimate Chess Puzzle Book with Gambit Editor Graham Burgess, the prospect of finding 1001 suitable puzzles seemed daunting to say the very least. Nevertheless, the challenge to produce such a large book of puzzles had already gripped me and there was no going back... Anyway, here I am, 1001 puzzles later and I’ve just about survived to tell the tale! The idea of the book was to come up with 1001 puzzles ranging from beginner to supergrandmaster difficulty. Naturally, I wanted to produce a book such that nearly all of the puzzles would be unfamiliar to the reader. I didn’t want to fall into the trap, as John Nunn so aptly put it, of taking a few positions from one puzzle book, a few from another, a pinch from a third and whisking them all together! In order to maximize the number of original puzzles, I have taken positions from three major sources. The first of these is the collection of my own games (I have well over a thousand of my own games on database). These games contain hitherto unpublished analysis and puzzles. In fact, selecting puzzle positions from this source was probably the easiest part of the book for me, as it was quite easy to remember games and variations that included suitable puzzles. In all there are around a hundred puzzles taken from my own games. The second and by far the biggest source were games that have been played over the past two years. Using in the main The Week in Chess and less so MegaBase 2000, I’ve sifted through tens of thousands of international games to find the vast majority of puzzle positions in this book. Indeed, many of the puzzles are from games actually played in this millennium! My third major source of puzzles appeared when I looked through games played at some old Soviet Championships. Such was the strength of these tournaments that these games are of very high quality. However, most of them will be unfamiliar to the majority of readers (when compared to,say, old World Championship games). This made it a fertile place to look for fresh positions. Now I should say something about the layout of the book. In Chapter 1 the reader is introduced to the tactical ideas and terms that are used here. It’s more of a glossary than anything else and it should help the reader when I talk about such things as decoys and interference later on. Chapter 2 contains over a hundred of what I term ‘level 1’puzzles. These puzzles are meant to be a starting block for those who have little experience in dealing with chess puzzles. Most of the ideas here include basic ways of winning material. Chapter 3 includes 66 checkmating puzzles, ranging from checkmates in one move on to more complex mating ideas. This chapter should also be useful to those with little experience. Chapter 4 is the largest in the book and includes 272 medium difficulty range puzzles (difficulty levels 2-4) . The range here is very wide. The easiest puzzles are still suitable for beginners and the most difficult ones could easily tax grandmasters. Chapter 5 contains the first group of five tests (further groups of five are contained in Chapters 8 and 10) . Each test contains 16 puzzles of a variety of difficulty, but when put together the tests are of roughly equivalent toughness. With 15 tests and 240 test positions overall, the reader can chart his or her progress throughout the book. Chapter 6 contains 86 puzzles from Soviet Championships from 1947-67. Generally the difficulty level of these puzzles range from level 2 to level 4. Chapter 7 is specially dedicated to those who suffer from ‘blunderitis’. I’ve borrowed the title of ‘Find the Wrong Move’ from John Nunn’s own puzzle book and have come up with 44 examples where the reader is invited to spot the blunder. Chapter 9 contains 80 endgame puzzles, with once again a difficulty range of level 2-4. Chapter 11 has been the most difficult chapter to produce and will also be the most difficult to solve. Here the puzzles range from level 5 upwards and I’m sure there are a few puzzles here that would tax even the strongest grandmaster. I wish you good luck in advance! Many people have been of assistance to me when writing this book. I would like to give special thanks to Chris Ward, Graham Burgess, Richard Palliser, Murugan Thiruchelvam, Paul Higgins, Sara Higgins and Nick Frost. }
*

{ (Bern 1992) } 1... Qg3
{ Black hits g2, e1 and g6 and wins material. 2 points for 1... Qg3!. } *

filename: World Champion Calculation Training - Part 1 Steinitz, Lasker, Capablanca, Alekhine_firstkey.pgn
solution:
{ Hello! My name is Mykhaylo Oleksiyenko. I am a GM and FIDE Trainer from Ukraine. My two biggest successes as a player have been winning the rapid championship of Ukraine in 2014 and, more importantly, winning the classical championship in 2016. My highest ELO was 2643 and I managed to win classical games against Vugar Gashimov, Pentala Harikrishna, Vasyl Ivanchuk, Radoslaw Wojtaszek, and many other strong GMs. I had the privilege of coaching the Dutch womens' chess team, the Egyptian national team, and the Ukrainian national team. I have also helped many strong players on an individual level: Amin Bassem, Ahmed Adly, Alexander Ipatov would be at the top of the list. In 2012, I managed to get my Candidate of physical and mathematical sciences
(that is the Ukrainian equivalent of a Ph.D.)
which concluded my mathematical journey. I still enjoy simple mathematical puzzles, logical puzzles, the theory of probability, and some physics of light and space. Enough about me let us talk about the course! You may wonder: why would I buy a course about tactics if there is so much free stuff on the internet? Well, three main reasons make this course stand out: 1. All the positions from the course happened in the games of World Champions. Most of the time the solution did not appear on the board. You will learn from the mistakes made by the best players of the time. 2. Each position has ONLY 1 winning option. I hate it when tactical puzzles have multiple solutions! There can, however, be occasional multiple solutions AFTER the first move. 3. Tactical blows do not appear out of thin air! There are certain signs that “tell you” to look for tactics. I will provide you with the exact questions that will help you focus your vision on what is important in the position. I will provide you with a list of things to pay attention to and I will refer to the list throughout the course. I checked EVERY single game of the first four World Champions
(more than 5000 in total!)
. I was looking specifically for tactical oversights. I used Stockfish for that, of course! There had to be only one best move in the position. Then I checked all the puzzles and made sure that they make sense from a human point of view
(as we all know, sometimes computers suggest moves that we cannot understand)
. At the beginning of each chapter, you will find relatively easy puzzles. Then the difficulty of the puzzle would rise until it goes to the hardest ones that could be challenging even for very strong players. You will find the combinations actually played by each Champion at the end of each chapter. The last position of each chapter is the most beautiful, from my subjective point of view. In this introductory chapter, I thoroughly analyze two games played in the World Championship matches. You will find some historical facts about the champions, some opening advice, some psychological advice, and some references to my first course for Chessable about the initiative in chess. }
1. -- *
